# Transcrição: 05-Promotional Campaigns Hack

- **Fonte:** 05-Promotional Campaigns Hack
- **Duração:** 08:44
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:06]

So when we think about promotions and we think about offers and discounts, I like to think about where the user is in their customer journey, how much they know about you as a brand or your product, how much you've connected the problem to the solution before I start thinking about discounts and offers. So I see lots of ads in my news feed of products and services I've never heard of before and they'll start with a discount. It may be that they want to get people in quick to experience how great their product or service is and that's fine. But if all you're doing at the top of the funnel is focusing on offers and getting people through on that basis, you can also attract the wrong people into your funnel. So there was a piece of analysis we did a couple of years ago now where we actually analyzed those that bought on discount versus those that didn't and the lifetime value of the non-discounters was higher and the repeat purchase rate was higher because they came through for a different reason. It wasn't that the discount tipped them in. For them it was we nailed the problem and solution and therefore we created a great experience as well. It may or may not be the same for you but it's just something to be conscious of of offering that discount too early. That said, there is a time and place for discounts, promotions, bundles, etc. We've talked for example about bundling products up. So if you buy one of this product, you can buy for this. If you buy two, you get an x% discount, etc. You could build upsells and cross-sells into your journey. But if you're looking for promotions, one of the great ways of doing this is to go to a voucher code website. They are literally a whole kind of gold mine full of information on all the different types of offers that you can offer plus what other retailers in your particular niche are offering. This example is looking at e-com for example but you can do the same in other niches as well. For example here, I've done a search for discount codes and I've got different discount sites that have loaded up. For wherever you are, obviously the sites will be tailored to your region. These are UK results. What I like to do is if I'm thinking of an idea for a promotion is to just click through on some of these sites. If I have a look at voucher codes, for example, this is their site at the moment. I get an idea for the top offers and I can do breakdowns by different trends, etc. So when you're stuck for ideas, a lot of them are 30% off, 20% off, etc. Sometimes there are bundles that are running. There's sites like Groupon, for example, if you're doing local advertising, they have a lot more local deals. You can see some kind of restaurant ideas, tech offers that are in place, home and garden, etc. So there's a whole bundle of offers and promotions you can tap into to give you an idea of what you can search for and what's being offered elsewhere. So let's say

## [03:06 - 06:07]

you're in the Apparel niche and you're looking for offer ideas. If you click through, often they show you a whole list of other offers as well. So right now, they're promoting this offer 30% off, men's shirts and dresses and men's shirts ends today. They've got student offers. So there's an idea. Like if you're looking for a promotion, sometimes it's worth niching down. So this is on particular categories, but this is based on a particular persona, an avatar that they've got, 15% student discount. So for example, if I was looking to do something like that, there's a few things I'd be looking at. So number one, what age of students, obviously, you'd maybe say 18 to 21 or maybe even up to 25. So university students do a promotion, target them in Facebook. And that's kind of one way of doing it. The other thing is looking at the timing as well. So for example, when do people start school? Generally, it's September time for most countries. When do they break? The obvious ones are around December and Christmas time and then Easter and summer and things like that. And then it gives you a hook to put on the end of it. So you could say 15% student summer discount, like get your shirts and dresses or whatever it is. And personally, I would split the targeting as well. So for example, I might do a 15% student offer for men and women. But in my marketing, I would separate the targeting so that I can customize my creative as well. So if I'm targeting women, I may choose different kinds of apparel that may appeal more to women than men and vice versa. But also that promotion brings in the right people based on them being students based on this discount, then getting them into your funnel and kind of thinking about a hook. So for example, I might have a summer student discount offer, which is talking about the upcoming summer holidays and you know, you want to go out and go go away with your friends or maybe you're planning a holiday to then tie in with the promotion and the reason for a discount. Like the worst thing is where you just say, Hey, we got 15% off just because. But when you make it specific to say 15% off all women's apparel for students, that now makes it specific. And then it makes it more attractive for me to think about that offer. So like when I'm tying in that promotion, I'm trying to think of making it as easy for my prospect to op themselves into this offer, and then thinking about how I can funnel them into that journey. So for example, for this, I would create a custom landing page, give it a summer feel, have some students or kind of younger people in my images, if possible, I would also go a step further. So for example, if I'm targeting 18 to 25, my message is all students. And let's say I've got lookalikes running, I'm doing some broad etc. Then I'd be thinking about what kind of

## [06:07 - 08:44]

additional targeting I could think about. What about people that are searching travel, like maybe there's some travel brands you can attach into in terms of interest targeting, I'd be running that through my graduation testing process to see if there's other opportunities. So someone who's a student, someone who's of this particular age range, someone that's interested in a discount, someone that's looking to go away in the summer, and then matching up interests related to that. In fact, I'd also go one step further and think about, for example, affiliate or partnership promotion. So affiliate marketing is great, you can join networks and you can join their programs and start tapping into hundreds, if not thousands of affiliates. But there's also no harm in contacting brands that have the similar customer to you as well. So for example, if I was Ted Baker, and I'm offering a 15% student discount for the summer for people that's looking to go away, then I might approach Expedia and say, hey, we've got this great promotion, we'll give you a fixed CPA. So we'll give you a fixed offer deal. So every purchase that's made, we give you, I don't know, $20. I'm making this up now. From Expedia's point of view, if they're guaranteeing $20 for every sale, maybe it's good, maybe it's not, that's something where you'd kind of negotiate. And I expect some like Expedia would need obviously more. But that now allows you to fix your costs. So with Facebook, you know that your CPA is variable, it's really dependent on so many factors. CPA deals with partnerships and promotions offers you so much more. So like, as you can see, I've just taken one discount. And I've literally just created that promotion on the spot right now, literally just by clicking into this particular brand, seeing this offer. And this is how I would come up with offers all the way through. And I'm kind of looking at niche, looking at previous offers offered, and coming up with my own ideas like free lipstick with handbag or purse orders over 150. I don't know, maybe that's good, maybe it's not something to test. If you have an accessory store, and you do have like these kind of products, there's no harm in copying it. It's your store, it's your promotions. Can you offer a free lipstick? Can you source one in? Is there something you can create that complements the product? There's just so many ways to look at discounts and promotions. But you know, if you're looking for an offer, and you're thinking about what you can create and kind of what's coming up, you know, these sites are perfect for research. So have a look at that, see if you can can be inspired, run that through your testing process and see what kind of results you can create from it.
